planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Korean stewartia resents root disturbance — do not move after planting. Site in a protected location away from hot afternoon sun and drying winds. Mulch to maintain even soil moisture.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Korean stewartia is a four-season specimen tree widely regarded as one of the most beautiful of all small ornamental trees. In summer, it produces large, white, camellia-like flowers with showy orange stamens over several weeks. As summer transitions to fall, the foliage turns brilliant shades of orange, red, and purple. In winter and spring, the intricate, flaking bark — peeling in irregular patches to reveal a mosaic of gray, orange, tan, and cinnamon — provides some of the most dramatic winter interest of any tree. The tree is pyramidal in youth and gradually broadens with age. More heat-tolerant than Japanese stewartia and slightly more adaptable to zone 8 conditions. One of the finest specimen trees for the transitional South, performing best in partial afternoon shade in zones 7–8.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic soil in full sun; pale pawpaw is a Florida/Georgia endemic of dry, sandy soils and sandhills — unlike common pawpaw (Asimina triloba), which prefers moist, rich bottomland, this species is adapted to xeric, nutrient-poor, sandy habitats in the lower South.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, sandy, slightly acidic, infertile soil in full sun; pale pawpaw is a shrubby member of the custard apple family endemic to dry sandhills, sandhill scrub, and dry oak-pine woodland of northern and central Florida and southern Georgia — a very different habitat than the moist bottomlands of common pawpaw (A. triloba). The white flowers with pale cream to white petals are produced before leaf emergence, showy against the bare stems in late winter to early spring. The small, oval fruits are edible and custard-flavored like common pawpaw but smaller. The larvae of the zebra swallowtail butterfly (Protographium marcellus) feed exclusively on Asimina species, making all pawpaws critically important for this distinctive butterfly. A rare and threatened species in its native Florida sandhill habitat, valuable for ecological restoration of dry, sandy, native ecosystems.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall immediately upon ripening (they lose viability quickly) in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; southern sugar maple is the heat-adapted southern relative of sugar maple, native to mixed hardwood forests of the coastal plain and piedmont from Virginia to Texas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; southern sugar maple is the heat-tolerant, summer-drought-adapted southern counterpart to the familiar sugar maple (Acer saccharum), differing in smaller leaf size, lighter texture, and significantly greater heat tolerance, performing well in zones 7–9 where northern sugar maple languishes or dies in the summer heat. The fall color is outstanding — rich orange, red, and yellow tones that rival northern sugar maple in many years — providing some of the best native fall color in the South. The bark is more furrowed and less smooth than northern maple. Although not tapped commercially for syrup, the sap does contain sugar. A superb native shade tree for southern gardens providing habitat, fall color, and a graceful canopy that suburban northern sugar maples cannot achieve in the Gulf South.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall at 3–4 times their diameter depth in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade; Henry's lily is more lime-tolerant and shade-tolerant than most lilies and is an important parent of the Oriental Trumpet lily hybrids. Plant where roots are shaded but flower stems reach light.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade; Henry's lily is a Chinese species of great horticultural importance — both as a garden plant in its own right and as a major parent in the development of the spectacular Oriental Trumpet (Orienpet) hybrid lilies. The tall stems bear many pendent, swept-back (turk's-cap-form) flowers of a warm apricot-orange with dark spots and prominent papillae on the inner surface. More shade-tolerant than most lilies and unusually lime-tolerant, thriving in neutral to slightly alkaline soils where most Asian lilies decline. An important structural plant for the summer border, producing large colonies when left undisturbed for many years. Named for Augustine Henry who collected the first specimens in Hubei province, China. Excellent in dappled shade at woodland edges with the roots shaded by other plants.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; fringed loosestrife spreads by rhizomes and seeds to form colonies in moist woodland edges, stream banks, and wet meadows. The cultivar 'Firecracker' has striking purple-bronze foliage.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun along stream banks, pond margins, rain gardens, and moist woodland edges; fringed loosestrife is a native North American species of the primrose family, producing starry, five-petaled, bright yellow flowers in midsummer that dangle from arching stems above broad, opposite leaves. The common name comes from the distinctive fringe of fine hairs on the leaf petioles. The cultivar 'Firecracker' has become popular in the ornamental trade for its striking, deep purple-bronze foliage that contrasts dramatically with the yellow flowers and adds unusual dark color to the moist shade garden throughout summer. Self-sows and spreads by rhizomes in appropriate moist conditions to form naturalistic colonies. Provides nectar for numerous small native bees and is a host plant for pollen-specialist bees of the genus Macropis.
